From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

10th US national census

| 1880 United States census | |
| --- | --- |
| ---   ← [1870](/wiki/1870_United_States_census "1870 United States census")  **June 1, 1880 (1880-06-01)**  [1890](/wiki/1890_United_States_census "1890 United States census") →   --- | |
| Seal of the Department of the Interior | |
| [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison") in the 1880 U.S. census | |
| General information | |
| Country | United States |
| Authority | [Census Office](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") |
| Results | |
| Total population | 50,189,209 (Increase 30.2%) |
| Most populous state | [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") 5,082,871 |
| Least populous state | [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") 62,266 |

The **1880 United States census**, conducted by the [Census Office](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") during June 1880, was the tenth [United States census](/wiki/United_States_census "United States census").[[1]](#cite_note-ipums-1) It was the first time that women were permitted to be [enumerators](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Special:Search/enumerator "wikt:Special:Search/enumerator").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Superintendent of the Census was [Francis Amasa Walker](/wiki/Francis_Amasa_Walker "Francis Amasa Walker").[[3]](#cite_note-3) This was the first census in which a city—[New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City")—recorded a population of over one million, and the first census in which the 20 most populated cities all recorded over 100,000 residents.

## Data collected

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1880_United_States_census&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Data collected")]

Five schedules were authorized by the 1880 Census Act, four of which were filled out by the enumerators:[[4]](#cite_note-cph-4)

* Schedule 1 (Population), which was similar to that used for the [previous census](/wiki/United_States_Census,_1870 "United States Census, 1870"), with a few exceptions.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* Schedule 2 (Mortality), which used the same inquiries as in 1870, and added inquiries to record [marital status](/wiki/Marital_status "Marital status"), birthplace of parents, length of residence in the United States or territory, and name of place where the disease was contracted, if other than place of death.
* Schedule 3 (Agriculture), which greatly expanded inquiries concerning various crops (including acreage for principal crop), and included questions on farm tenure, weeks of hired labor, annual cost for fence building and repair, fertilizer purchases, and the number of livestock.
* Schedule 5 (Manufacturing), which expanded to include information on the greatest number of hands employed at any time during the year, the number of hours in the ordinary work day from May to November and November to May, the average daily wages paid to skilled mechanics and laborers, months of full-and part-time operation, and machinery used.

Schedule 4 (Social statistics) was the responsibility of experts and special agents, rather than the enumerators.[[4]](#cite_note-cph-4) The majority of the data came from correspondence with officials of institutions providing care and treatment of certain members of the population. Experts and special agents also were employed to collect data on valuation, taxation, and indebtedness; religion and libraries; colleges, academies, and schools; newspapers and periodicals, and wages.[[4]](#cite_note-cph-4)

Special agents were also charged with collecting data on specific industries throughout the country, and included the manufactures of iron and steel; cotton, woolen, and [worsted](/wiki/Worsted "Worsted") goods; silk and silk goods; chemical products and salt; coke and glass; shipbuilding; and all aspects of fisheries and mining, including the production of coal and petroleum.[[4]](#cite_note-cph-4)

Full documentation for the 1880 population census, including census forms and enumerator instructions, is available from the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series"), which contains microdata.

## Data availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1880_United_States_census&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Data availability")]

The original census enumeration sheets were [microfilmed](/wiki/Microform "Microform") by the Census Bureau; after which the original sheets were transferred to various state archives, libraries, or universities.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The microfilmed census is available in rolls from the [National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration"). Several organizations also host images of the microfilmed census online, along which digital indices.

[Microdata](/wiki/Microdata_(statistics) "Microdata (statistics)") from the 1880 population census are freely available through the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series"). [Aggregate data](/wiki/Aggregate_data "Aggregate data") for small areas, together with compatible cartographic boundary files, can be downloaded from the [National Historical Geographic Information System](/wiki/National_Historical_Geographic_Information_System "National Historical Geographic Information System").

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1880_United_States_census&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Results")]

The 1880 census determined the resident population of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") to be 50,189,209, an increase of 30.2 percent over the 38,555,983 persons [enumerated](/wiki/Enumeration "Enumeration") during the [1870 census](/wiki/United_States_Census,_1870 "United States Census, 1870").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The [mean center of United States population](/wiki/Mean_center_of_United_States_population "Mean center of United States population") for 1880 was in [Boone County, Kentucky](/wiki/Boone_County,_Kentucky "Boone County, Kentucky").

The results from the census were [used to determine](/wiki/Apportionment_Bill "Apportionment Bill") the [apportionment](/wiki/United_States_congressional_apportionment "United States congressional apportionment") for the [48th](/wiki/48th_United_States_Congress "48th United States Congress"), [49th](/wiki/49th_United_States_Congress "49th United States Congress"), [50th](/wiki/50th_United_States_Congress "50th United States Congress"), [51st](/wiki/51st_United_States_Congress "51st United States Congress"), and [52nd](/wiki/52nd_United_States_Congress "52nd United States Congress") sessions of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress").

The processing of the 1880 census data took so long (eight years) that the Census Bureau contracted [Herman Hollerith](/wiki/Herman_Hollerith "Herman Hollerith") to design and build a [tabulating machine](/wiki/Tabulating_machine "Tabulating machine") to be used for the [next census](/wiki/United_States_Census,_1890 "United States Census, 1890").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) The 1880 census also led to the discovery of the [Alabama paradox](/wiki/Alabama_paradox "Alabama paradox").

Source: Table I, Population of the United States, by States and Territories[[10]](#cite_note-10)
